title: 6.8 practice
description: Practice what you learned in lesson 6.8!
topic_path: The First Global Age (1200 to 1750 CE)

assessment_items.question_content:
**Why were the diseases introduced by migrating Europeans so deadly to indigenous communities?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Indigenous communities lacked immunity to unfamiliar diseases.
They had poor infrastructure for caring for the sick.
They were more susceptible due to malnourishment.
They were exposed multiple ways through food, air, and physical contact.

assessment_items.question_content:
**What were some of the environmental benefits of the epidemics in the Americas post-European migration?** 

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: growing forests and repopulation of hunted animals
cleaner water and productive farmland
soil degradation and less biodiversity
urban development and expansion of cities

assessment_items.question_content:
**In what way did animals from Afro-Eurasia significantly change life in the Americas during the Columbian Exchange?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Horses increased mobility and transformed hunting and warfare for many Indigenous groups.
Chickens led to the development of large-scale poultry farms across Indigenous territories.
Pigs and cows replaced all traditional food sources for Indigenous peoples.
The introduction of animals caused Indigenous people to abandon agriculture.

assessment_items.question_content:
**What was one major consequence of bringing European cash crops, such as tobacco and sugar, to the Americas?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: increased demand for enslaved labor
a rapid decline in agricultural production
the end of native farming traditions
a complete ban on American-grown crops

assessment_items.question_content:
**What was one major negative impact of introducing European crops and animals to the American ecosystem?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Native habitats were destroyed and indigenous plant species were pushed out.
Indigenous animals died from diseases carried by European livestock.
The introduction of European species increased biodiversity.
Soil conditions improved, making farming easier across the continent.

assessment_items.question_content:
**While the potato is generally seen as having a positive impact on Europe during the Columbian Exchange, what was one negative consequence of its introduction?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The dependency on a single crop resulted in famines when the potato crop failed.
It caused widespread soil depletion and erosion.
It introduced new pests that destroyed other important European crops.
It reduced the diversity of European livestock by replacing animal farming.

assessment_items.question_content:
**How did the introduction of sugarcane affect the population demographics in the Americas?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: It resulted in the enslavement of large numbers of African people to work on plantations.
It led to a significant increase in Indigenous populations due to improved nutrition.
It caused a decline in European immigration to the Americas.
It encouraged the growth of small-scale family farms rather than large plantations.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the following best describes the conditions experienced by enslaved laborers on sugarcane plantations in the Americas?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Enslaved workers were treated as property, suffered under brutal working conditions and cruel punishments.
Plantation owners often treated enslaved laborers as paid employees with rights and freedoms.
Enslaved workers were primarily used for skilled labor and rarely participated in fieldwork.
Enslaved workers were granted small plots of land and worked limited hours under supervision.

assessment_items.question_content:
**What demographic changes occurred in Africa as a result of the Columbian Exchange?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: A significant loss of population due to the transatlantic slave trade.
Rapid population growth due to the arrival of new European settlers.
Increased urbanization as African kingdoms expanded into industrial centers.
Widespread migration of African communities to the Americas by choice.

assessment_items.question_content:
>The slaves are worked hard during the whole day. When the bell rings at ten or eleven o’clock at night, they go to their rest. At four in the morning the bell rings again, and they are off to the field. This continues without intermission during the crop.

>— James Ramsay, 18th-century abolitionist


**What does this passage reveal about the experiences of enslaved laborers on sugar plantations in the Caribbean?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Enslaved people endured long, exhausting hours with little rest, especially during the harvest.
Enslaved workers were allowed generous time for rest and recovery during the harvest season.
Plantation owners provided enslaved laborers with daily education and personal time.
The excerpt shows that enslaved laborers had flexible schedules based on weather and crop needs.

assessment_items.question_content:
The diagram below illustrates the transfer of goods and diseases during the Columbian Exchange. 

[[☃ image 1]]

**Based on the diagram, which of the following best explains how the Columbian Exchange affected global agriculture?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.alt: Arrows show two-way exchanges between the Americas and Europe, Africa, and Asia. From the Americas, goods like corn, beans, potatoes, tomatoes, vanilla, cocoa, and turkeys were sent to Europe, Africa, and Asia. From Europe, Africa, and Asia, goods like grains, grapes, bananas, citrus fruits, sugarcane, livestock (cattle, pigs, sheep, horses), honeybees, and diseases (like smallpox, influenza, and measles) were brought to the Americas.

choices.content: It introduced staple crops like potatoes and maize to Europe, which increased food supplies and population growth.
It caused a collapse of farming systems across Afro-Eurasia due to crop diseases.
It eliminated traditional crops in the Americas, replacing them entirely with European plants.
It limited food options across continents by reducing biodiversity.

assessment_items.question_content:
The image below depicts events after the arrival of Europeans in the Americas.

[[☃ image 1]]

**Based on the image, what was one major demographic consequence of the Columbian Exchange for Indigenous populations in the Americas?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.alt: Black-and-white illustration from a sixteenth-century Aztec codex showing the effects of smallpox after European contact. The image depicts several Indigenous people lying on woven mats, covered with sores, while another person appears to tend to one of the sick.

choices.content: Exposure to Old World diseases like smallpox caused widespread death and population decline.
Indigenous groups began migrating to Europe in large numbers to escape disease.
The introduction of new foods helped Indigenous populations grow rapidly.
Indigenous communities gained access to advanced European medicine, improving life expectancy.
